text.title: Change Control Process
text.summary: Six-step change control cycle from request capture to baseline update, with tiered decision authority and mandatory impact analysis areas.

section.title: Process Flow
section.p: Every change request follows: Request → Log → Analyze → Decide → Implement → Close. No step is skippable; informal approval is not approval.

rule.statement: The agent prepares the impact packet; a human approver signs off before implementation begins — never auto-approve.
rule.rationale: Change approval has organizational and contractual consequences. Automated approval removes the accountability trace that auditors, sponsors, and clients require.

list.item: Minor: < 1 day impact, < $500 cost → Project Manager approves
Medium: 1-5 days impact, < $5,000 cost → Sponsor approves
Major: > 5 days OR > $5,000 OR high-risk → CCB approves
Any CR that increases risk score above threshold → CCB regardless of cost/days

rule.statement: Tier the process by impact size; a 2-week CCB cadence for minor changes creates informal workarounds.
rule.rationale: When the formal process is too slow, requesters route around it. Informal "just do it" approvals reappear as scope creep. Minor changes approved at PM level maintain process speed without bypassing governance.

list.item: Scope — what features, deliverables, or WBS elements are affected?
Schedule — how many working days added or removed?
Cost — what additional budget is required?
Quality — does the change affect standards, testing coverage, or technical debt?
Risk — what new risks does the change introduce?
Resources — which team members must work on it, and what does that displace?

rule.statement: Run impact analysis against current baselines (scope, schedule, budget), not against the original plan.
rule.rationale: The original plan is stale. Impact must be measured from the current approved baseline so the delta is accurate. Comparing to the original plan understates accumulated drift.

section.title: Change Register Status Values

list.item: Under Review — impact analysis in progress
Approved — will be implemented; baselines must be updated before work starts
Rejected — will not be implemented; reason documented for repeat-CR detection
Deferred — postponed to future phase or release

rule.statement: Track rejected CRs forever; use them to detect repeat asks and as evidence for descope discussions.
rule.rationale: Rejected requests rarely die — they re-submit under a new title. A history of rejections with documented reasons allows the PM to identify repeat patterns and address the root demand.

section.reference: CR form with description, justification, impact table, options, decision block
Change register table for tracking all CRs
Assign approval tier from impact YAML
